content.problemStatement:
For the lowercase English letter α\alphaα, let ord(α)\text{ord}(\alpha)ord(α) denote the order of α\alphaα in the alphabet, under the standard ordering a,b,c,…,za, b, c, \ldots, za,b,c,…,z.

For example, ord(b)=2,ord(j)=10,\text{ord}(b) = 2, \text{ord}(j) = 10,ord(b)=2,ord(j)=10, and ord(z)=26\text{ord}(z) = 26ord(z)=26.

You are given a string SSS containing NNN lowercase English letters.

You can perform the following 222 operations on it:


Choose a subsequence of the string that equals "back"\texttt{"back"}"back", and delete the subsequence from SSS.

Then, choose any character α\alphaα such that ord(α)\text{ord}(\alpha)ord(α) has **at most** two divisors, and insert α\alphaα at the start of SSS.

Note that this operation reduces the length of SSS by 333.


Choose a subsequence of the string that equals "front"\texttt{"front"}"front", and delete the subsequence from SSS.

Then, choose any character α\alphaα such that ord(α)\text{ord}(\alpha)ord(α) has **strictly more than** two divisors, and append α\alphaα to SSS.

Note that this operation reduces the length of SSS by 444.


You may perform these two operations as many times as you like (possibly, zero), and in any order.

Find the **minimum** possible length of the final string after doing so.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the size of the string.
The second line of each test case contains the string SSS, consisting of lowercase English letters.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the minimum possible length of resulting string.
constraints: SSS contains only lowercase English letters, i.e, the characters `a, b, c, ..., z`.
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 3⋅1053\cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Consider the following sequence of operations:

Initially, S="efmbagrowcnukpt"S = \texttt{"efmbagrowcnukpt"}S="efmbagrowcnukpt".
Indices 4,5,10,134, 5, 10, 134,5,10,13 form a subsequence equal to "back"\texttt{"back"}"back".
Delete that, and insert qqq at the start of SSS. Note that ord(q)=17\text{ord}(q) = 17ord(q)=17, which has only two divisors.
The new string is S="qefmgrownupt"S = \texttt{"qefmgrownupt"}S="qefmgrownupt".
Now, the indices 3,6,7,9,123,6,7,9,123,6,7,9,12 form a subsequence equal to "front"\texttt{"front"}"front".
Delete it and append ddd to SSS, turning it into "qemgwupd"\texttt{"qemgwupd"}"qemgwupd".

SSS now has length 888. It can be shown that reaching a smaller length is not possible.
